title: First pyrography burn on birch
subtitle: A botanical leaf outline burned onto a prepared birch panel using a solid-tip burner
excerpt: A first pyrography burn on birch teaches the basics of line speed, pressure, and tip temperature with one of the most forgiving species available. Birch has a pale, close-grained surface that shows burned lines clearly and responds consistently. A simple botanical motif of one or two overlapping leaves is enough to cover all the fundamental burns: straight lines, gentle curves, and hatched shading.
sourceNotes: Pyrography on birch and other light-coloured woods documented in late Victorian decorative craft traditions; technique consistent through 20th century pyrography instruction manuals.

content.text: , and pressure. A first session on birch is about tuning all three. Burn test lines across the panel at different settings to see the range of marks before you start the design. Birch reacts the same way across the panel, which makes it the right first wood. Pine has hard and soft bands of grain that give patchy results on a first try.

A solid birch panel or plaque, 20 cm x 15 cm, 6 to 8 mm thick. Birch plaques for pyrography are widely available from craft suppliers. The surface must be solid birch, not birch plywood with synthetic binders; plywood can produce toxic fumes when burned. Lime and sycamore at the same dimensions are equally suitable: both are pale and close-grained.
A solid-tip pyrography burner with a variable temperature control. A

(a rounded-point or ball tip) for outline and line work. A cooling stand for the burner between passes. Sandpaper to 240 grit for surface preparation. Tracing paper and a soft pencil for transferring the design.

A first motif should be simple: one or two overlapping leaves with visible veins, at a scale that fills most of the panel. Draw or trace the design on paper. Choose a leaf with clear, legible veins: oak, sycamore, or horse chestnut all produce distinct, easy-to-follow lines. The outline and the main veins are burned as lines; the space between veins is shaded with
to distinguish foreground and background leaves.

Sand the birch panel to 240 grit along the grain. Remove all sanding dust with a clean, dry cloth. Any grain fibres left standing will catch the tip and produce jagged lines; the surface must be smooth and dust-free before burning.

to place the leaf motif on the panel. Secure the traced design face-down on the panel with tape, then burnish firmly over the pencil lines with a blunt tool; the graphite transfers to the wood surface as a pale guide line. Lift the paper carefully and check all lines have transferred before removing completely.
Step 3: Calibrate the burner on scrap

Before touching the design, test lines on a scrap of the same birch panel. Set the temperature to medium and burn a series of straight lines at different speeds: one line very slowly, one at medium pace, one quickly. The results show the range of darkness available at this temperature. Adjust the temperature dial until the medium-speed line produces a warm golden-brown mark; this is the starting point for outline work.

Start the writing tip at one end of an outline and draw it smoothly along the transferred guide line at the calibrated speed. Keep the arm moving at the same pace throughout each line. Lift the tip at the end of each pass and return it to the cooling stand before repositioning on the next line. On a curve, pivot the wrist rather than rotating the panel; consistent wrist movement produces smoother arcs than moving the wood.

Burn the main central vein first, then the secondary veins branching from it. Use the same medium setting; veins are typically the same darkness as the outline. The veins taper toward their tips; raise line speed slightly as the tip approaches each vein tip to produce a natural tapering effect.

Shade the sections of the back leaf (the one that appears behind the other) with diagonal hatching lines: closely spaced parallel burns at about 45 degrees, spaced 2 to 3 mm apart. Reduce the temperature by one setting for shading work; softer hatching lines read as a lighter tone than the outline and create the depth effect. Add cross-hatching (a second layer of hatching at 90 degrees to the first) to the darkest areas if more depth is needed.

A pyrography panel is left unfinished or sealed with a light coat of beeswax or clear wax. Do not apply linseed oil to a pyrography panel: the oil darkens the wood around the burned lines and reduces contrast. If a backing is wanted, glue a rectangle of dark felt to the back.
Try a single fern frond on a tall panel: the repeating frond pinnules are good practice for consistent hatching rhythm.
Add lettering below the leaf motif: burn a short text or name as a first attempt at letter forms. Simple serif letters are easier to keep consistent than sans-serif.
Use a spoon blank as the panel. Burning a design onto the handle of a spoon before oiling pairs pyrography with woodcarving in one piece.

attrs.intro: Problems on a first pyrography session.

items.fix: Practice constant line speed on scrap before returning to the design. The hand must move at a consistent pace from start to end of each line with no acceleration or hesitation.
items.cause: Line speed varied during the burn; slower sections are darker and wider.
items.symptom: Lines are dark and wide in some places and faint and narrow in others

items.fix: Sand the surface more carefully to 240 grit before burning. Check the tip is clean: a clogged tip snags. Wipe the tip on a damp cloth (not wet) between passes to remove carbon build-up.
items.cause: The surface has a raised grain fibre the tip caught, or the tip was dragged across a grain boundary at too shallow an angle.
items.symptom: The tip snags and produces a ragged spot rather than a clean line

items.fix: Keep the temperature setting constant during shading. Do not rest the burner between hatching lines; work the whole shading area in one continuous session without allowing the tip to cool.
items.cause: Line speed was inconsistent across the hatching pass, or the temperature changed between passes.
items.symptom: The hatching shading looks uneven with some lines much darker than others

attrs.heading: Common problems
